messages.content: rewrite the following sentence and make it more professional "A limitation of LOF screens is that accessible candidates are confined within the cellular transcriptome under certain settings, and may miss some key immune regulators in human cancers. Gain-of-function (GOF) screens can potentially discover the genetic regulators that may not normally be active in the used cells but promote the phenotype. "

One constraint of LOF screening methodologies is their limited ability to identify immune regulators essential for human cancers that may not be present in the cellular transcriptome under specific conditions. On the other hand, GOF screening techniques have the potential to uncover genetic regulators that are typically inactive in the utilized cells yet contribute to the observed phenotype.

High-throughput CRISPR screens are powerful tools to discover the key regulators associated with immunotherapy response10,11. Recently, lots of functional screens under in vitro or in vivo immunotherapy settings have uncovered many novel regulators of immune evasion and immunotherapy responses.

High-throughput CRISPR screens represent a robust strategy to identify the essential regulators linked to immunotherapy response. In recent times, a plethora of functional screens conducted under in vitro or in vivo immunotherapy frameworks have successfully uncovered numerous novel regulators associated with immune evasion and immunotherapy responses.

Notably, majority of CRISPR screens are loss-of-function (LOF) screens. A limitation of LOF screens is the limited ability to identify cancer immune regulators that may not expressed by the used cells under the experimental conditions.

It is worth noting that a considerable proportion of CRISPR screens focus on loss-of-function (LOF) methodologies. However, a constraint of LOF screens is their restricted capacity to determine cancer immune regulators that may not be expressed by the cells utilized under the experimental conditions.

rewrite to make more professional "High-throughput CRISPR screens represent a robust strategy to identify the essential regulators for immunotherapy response10,11. Recently, a plethora of functional screens are conducted under in vitro or in vivo immunotherapy settings and have successfully uncovered many novel regulators associated with immune evasion and immunotherapy responses. It is worth noting that majority of the studies are loss-of-function (LOF) screens with CRISPR knockout. However, a limitation of LOF screens is the restricted ability to identify cancer immune regulators that may not expressed by the used cells under the experimental conditions. On the other hand, gain-of-function (GOF) screens have the potential to uncover the phenotype regulators even those that are not inactive in the used cells. In another word, GOF screens can potentially identify any genes that promote or dampens T cell killings including the genes not expressed by the used cell lines. Recently, GOF screens have been adopted to identify functional genes involved in targeted therapy resistance and T cell biology12–14."

High-throughput CRISPR screens have emerged as a robust strategy for the identification of essential regulators of immunotherapy response10,11. Recently, several functional screens conducted under in vitro or in vivo immunotherapy frameworks have been successful in identifying various novel regulators associated with immune evasion and immunotherapy responses. It is important to note that the majority of studies utilize loss-of-function (LOF) screens using CRISPR knockout methods. However, a significant limitation of LOF screens is their restricted capacity to identify cancer immune regulators that may not be expressed by the cells used under experimental conditions. Conversely, gain-of-function (GOF) screens possess the potential to identify phenotype regulators, including those not actively expressed in the utilized cell lines, that promote or attenuate T-cell cytotoxicity. Consequently, GOF screens represent a promising approach for the detection of genes associated with targeted therapy resistance and T-cell biology12-14.
GOF screens using CRISPR activation technology have been adopted to identify functional genes involved in targeted therapy resistance and T cell biology12–14
CRISPR activation technology has been employed to conduct gain-of-function (GOF) screens aimed at the identification of functional genes linked to targeted therapy resistance and T-cell biology12-14.

To identify key immune regulators mediating T cell killing resistance, we applied an in vivo GOF screen strategy to identify the regulators mediating tumor resistance to T cell therapy in the primary and metastatic tumor settings. From these screens, we discovered aberrant expression of Transient Receptor Potential Cation Channel Subfamily V Members 1-6 (TRPV1-6) as a previously uncharacterized evasion pathway of T cell killing. Subsequent validation experiments using human cancer tissue confirmed that  aberrant expression of TRPV channels is commonly exist in cancers. Knockout or Targeting the aberrantly expressed TRPV channels especially TRPV6 re-sensitizes immunotherapy unresponsive breast and prostate cancer to T cell therapy, further substantiate the role of TRPV channels in mediating tumor evasion of T cell therapy.

In order to identify the key immune regulators associated with T-cell killing resistance, we utilized an in vivo GOF screening approach to identify regulators responsible for tumor resistance to T-cell therapy in both primary and metastatic tumor settings. Our screenings led to the discovery of aberrant expression of Transient Receptor Potential Cation Channel Subfamily V Members 1-6 (TRPV1-6) as a previously uncharacterized pathway for T-cell killing evasion. Subsequent validation studies using human cancer tissue confirmed the common existence of TRPV channel aberrant expression in cancer. Notably, knockout or targeting of the aberrantly expressed TRPV channels, particularly TRPV6, resulted in resensitization of immunotherapy unresponsive breast and prostate cancer to T-cell therapy, thereby providing further evidence supporting the role of TRPV channels in mediating tumor evasion of T-cell therapy.

Albeit checkpoint immunotherapies have achieved great clinical success, majority of the patients still cannot benefit from these immunotherapies, highlighting the importance of uncovering new immune evasion and immunotherapy resistance pathways. Cytotoxic CD8+ T cells are the major killers during immunosurveillance and cancer immunotherapy. Since the clinical success of engineered chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) T cells (CAR-T cells) in the treatment of hematologic tumors such as leukemia, lymphoma and multiple myeloma1–4, extensive efforts have been devoted to developing new T cell therapies5–9. However, malignant tumors especially solid tumors can use a variety of immune-regulatory mechanisms or biological pathways to escape the surveillance and killing of T cell. Therefore, the discovery of new immune escape or T cell therapy-resistant pathways is essential for developing new single or combinatorial immunotherapies.

Despite the considerable clinical success of checkpoint immunotherapies, a significant proportion of patients still do not receive any benefits from these treatments. However, malignant tumors, especially solid tumors, can utilize an array of immune-regulatory mechanisms or biological pathways to evade T-cell surveillance and killing. Therefore, the discovery of novel immune escape or T-cell therapy-resistant pathways is vital for the development of new single or combinatorial immunotherapies.
